title: Net Unrealized Appreciation (NUA) Tax Calculator
summary: Calculates the total tax liability for Net Unrealized Appreciation (NUA) of employer stock from a qualified retirement plan, applying capital gains tax to NUA and ordinary income tax to the cost basis.
selection_hints: Ordinary income tax on cost basis
applicable_when: Calculating tax on appreciated employer stock from a qualified retirement plan.
Distributing employer stock in a lump sum from a 401(k) or similar plan.
Seeking favorable capital gains tax rates on the appreciated portion of employer stock.
The distribution meets NUA eligibility requirements.
not_applicable_when: The employer stock distribution does not qualify for NUA treatment (e.g., not a lump sum, no qualifying event).
Rolling employer stock into an IRA (NUA rules generally don't apply).
Calculating general capital gains on stock held in a regular brokerage account.
The stock value is less than its cost basis (indicating a loss, not appreciation).
Only seeking to calculate tax on ordinary retirement plan distributions.
scope_boundaries: Applies only to employer stock from qualified retirement plans.
Calculates tax for the NUA portion and the cost basis separately.
Assumes the distribution qualifies for NUA treatment.
Requires market value, cost basis, capital gains rate, and income tax rate inputs.

common_confusions.label: NUA Eligibility
common_confusions.difference: This calculation assumes NUA eligibility criteria are met (e.g., lump-sum distribution, qualifying event). If conditions are not met, the entire distribution may be taxed as ordinary income or rolled over.

common_confusions.label: NUA vs. Regular Retirement Distribution Tax
common_confusions.difference: NUA provides a specific tax advantage for employer stock, taxing appreciation at capital gains rates. Regular retirement distributions are typically taxed entirely as ordinary income.

common_confusions.label: NUA vs. Stock Sale in Brokerage Account
common_confusions.difference: NUA applies specifically to employer stock from qualified retirement plans upon distribution. Stock sold in a regular brokerage account is subject to standard capital gains rules.

title: Implied Volatility Calculation (Black-Scholes Model)
summary: Computes the implied volatility of a European call or put option by inverting the Black-Scholes option pricing model, given its market price and other option parameters.
applicable_when: Determining the implied volatility of a European option.
You have the market price of a European call or put option.
Using the Black-Scholes option pricing framework.
Analyzing option contracts where dividends are not explicitly modeled.
not_applicable_when: Pricing American options (due to early exercise possibility).
Options on assets with discrete dividends (without dividend adjustments).
When using models other than Black-Scholes (e.g., binomial tree, Monte Carlo).
For exotic options with complex payoffs.
scope_boundaries: Requires market price, stock price, strike price, time to expiration (in years), and risk-free rate.
Option type ('call' or 'put') can be specified; 'call' is the default.
Input values must be positive, and the risk-free rate should be within a plausible range (e.g., -10% to 100%).
Calculates implied volatility as a percentage.
Assumes a continuously compounded risk-free rate.

common_confusions.label: European vs. American Options
common_confusions.difference: This model is for European options only, which can only be exercised at expiration, unlike American options which can be exercised any time up to expiration.

common_confusions.label: Call vs. Put Option
common_confusions.difference: Specify 'call' or 'put' as the option type; the default is 'call' if not provided.

common_confusions.label: Continuously vs. Annually Compounded Rate
common_confusions.difference: The Black-Scholes model uses a continuously compounded risk-free rate. If an annually compounded rate is provided, it must be converted.

common_confusions.label: Implied vs. Historical Volatility
common_confusions.difference: Implied volatility is derived from the option's market price, reflecting market expectations of future volatility, whereas historical volatility is based on past price movements.

title: Out-of-Pocket Cost Calculation for Healthcare
summary: Calculates the policyholder's total out-of-pocket cost for a healthcare service, considering their deductible, coinsurance, and the annual out-of-pocket maximum.
applicable_when: Estimating personal healthcare costs for a specific medical service.
Determining the remaining balance for a medical bill after insurance.
Understanding the financial impact of deductibles, coinsurance, and out-of-pocket maximums.
Assessing how close one is to reaching their annual out-of-pocket limit.
not_applicable_when: Calculating health insurance premiums.
Determining costs for services not covered by insurance.
Estimating costs for out-of-network healthcare providers.
Calculating total household medical expenses across multiple individuals with separate out-of-pocket limits.
When only a fixed copayment applies without deductible or coinsurance.
scope_boundaries: Calculates costs for a single healthcare event or aggregated services contributing to a single out-of-pocket calculation.
Assumes all billed services are covered by the health insurance plan.
Assumes all healthcare services are provided by in-network providers.
Requires inputs for total billed amount, deductible, coinsurance rate, and out-of-pocket maximum.
Applies to individual out-of-pocket limits.

common_confusions.label: Premiums
common_confusions.difference: This card calculates cost-sharing for services, not the regular monthly or annual premiums paid to maintain insurance coverage.

common_confusions.label: Copayments
common_confusions.difference: While copayments often count towards the out-of-pocket maximum, this calculation focuses on deductible and coinsurance. If only a copay applies, this card might not be the most direct fit.

common_confusions.label: Out-of-network costs
common_confusions.difference: This calculation primarily applies to in-network services. Out-of-network costs typically do not count towards your in-network out-of-pocket maximum.

common_confusions.label: Non-covered services
common_confusions.difference: Costs for services not covered by your health insurance plan do not count towards the out-of-pocket maximum and are not directly calculated here.

common_confusions.label: Total Annual Healthcare Spending
common_confusions.difference: This card calculates the out-of-pocket cost for a specific service or event, not your total annual healthcare spending, which might include premiums or non-covered expenses.

title: Nanny Tax
summary: Calculates the total federal nanny tax liability, encompassing both employer and employee shares of Social Security and Medicare taxes, plus federal unemployment tax (FUTA), based on annual wages paid to a household employee.
applicable_when: Calculating federal tax liability for household employees
Hiring a nanny, babysitter, or other domestic worker
Determining employer and employee share of Social Security and Medicare taxes for household wages
Calculating Federal Unemployment Tax (FUTA) for household employees
When total cash wages paid to a household employee exceed federal thresholds
not_applicable_when: Employee is a spouse, parent, or child under 18 (not primarily working as household help)
Household help hired through an employment agency
Calculating only state-level nanny or household employment taxes
Determining taxes for independent contractors (not household employees)
scope_boundaries: Focuses exclusively on federal nanny tax components: Social Security, Medicare, and FUTA
Calculates both employer and employee shares of Social Security and Medicare taxes
Does not include state or local employment taxes
Assumes the individual is classified as a household employee, not an independent contractor
Based on annual wages paid to a single household employee

common_confusions.label: Federal vs. State Nanny Tax
common_confusions.difference: This calculation covers federal Social Security, Medicare, and FUTA taxes only; it does not include any state or local nanny taxes.

common_confusions.label: Household Employee vs. Independent Contractor
common_confusions.difference: This tax applies only to household employees as defined by the IRS, not to independent contractors.

common_confusions.label: Nanny Tax Applicability Thresholds
common_confusions.difference: The tax is generally only applicable if cash wages meet specific annual thresholds (e.g., $2,300 for Social Security and Medicare); this card calculates the tax amount given rates and wages, not applicability based on thresholds.

title: Hindenburg Omen
summary: Analyzes market conditions to determine if the Hindenburg Omen signal is active, indicating a heightened probability of a stock market downturn or crash. It evaluates specific criteria related to 52-week highs/lows, market trend, and the McClellan Oscillator.
applicable_when: When seeking a technical signal for potential stock market crashes.
When analyzing market breadth and momentum indicators like 52-week highs/lows and McClellan Oscillator.
When the market is in an uptrend but showing signs of internal weakness.
When evaluating short-term market risk for approximately 30 days.
not_applicable_when: When analyzing individual stock performance or specific sectors.
When seeking long-term investment strategies or fundamental analysis.
When the market does not exhibit simultaneous high numbers of new 52-week highs and lows.
When a market uptrend is not present or the McClellan Oscillator is positive.
scope_boundaries: Focuses on the broad stock market index, not individual securities.
Primarily a short-term signal, relevant for about 30 trading days.
Considers specific criteria: percentage of 52-week highs/lows, market index uptrend, and McClellan Oscillator value.
Designed to predict increased probability of a downturn, not a guaranteed outcome.

common_confusions.label: Guaranteed market crash prediction
common_confusions.difference: The Hindenburg Omen is an indicator of increased probability for a downturn, not a certainty, and has a history of false alarms.

common_confusions.label: Individual stock indicator
common_confusions.difference: This omen applies to the overall stock market or a broad market index, not to the performance of single stocks.

common_confusions.label: Long-term market outlook
common_confusions.difference: The signal is generally considered relevant for a short period, typically around 30 trading days, and does not provide long-term forecasts.

title: Cash Flow from Operating Activities (CFO)
summary: Calculates Cash Flow from Operating Activities (CFO) using the indirect method. It adjusts net income for non-cash expenses like depreciation and amortization, tax adjustments, and changes in working capital components such as accounts receivable, liabilities, and inventories, to determine the cash generated by a company's core business operations.
applicable_when: Calculating cash generated by core business operations.
Analyzing a company's operational efficiency and liquidity.
Using the indirect method to prepare a cash flow statement.
Adjusting net income from an accrual basis to a cash basis for operating activities.
Evaluating a company's ability to generate cash internally.
not_applicable_when: Calculating cash flow using the direct method.
Analyzing cash flows from investing activities.
Analyzing cash flows from financing activities.
When only interested in net income (accrual basis profit).
scope_boundaries: Includes: Net income, depreciation, amortization, tax adjustments, changes in accounts receivable, changes in liabilities, changes in inventories, changes in other operating activities.
Excludes: Cash flows from investing activities (e.g., purchase/sale of assets), cash flows from financing activities (e.g., debt, equity, dividends).
Method: Specifically uses the indirect method.
Focus: Core, ongoing business operations.

common_confusions.label: Direct vs. Indirect Method
common_confusions.difference: This formula specifically applies the indirect method, starting with net income. It does not calculate CFO using the direct method which tracks individual cash receipts and payments.

common_confusions.label: Input convention for working capital changes
common_confusions.difference: The inputs for working capital changes (e.g., 'changes_in_accounts_receivable') are expected to represent the direct cash flow impact (e.g., a $15,000 increase in AR is entered as -15,000). If raw period-over-period changes are provided, the calculation will be incorrect without adjustment.

common_confusions.label: CFO vs. Funds From Operations (FFO)
common_confusions.difference: Funds From Operations (FFO) adjusts net income for non-cash expenses, but CFO further incorporates changes in working capital, providing a more complete picture of operating cash.

common_confusions.label: CFO vs. Net Income
common_confusions.difference: Net Income is an accrual-based measure of profitability, while CFO represents actual cash generated from operations, which can differ significantly due to non-cash items and working capital changes.

title: Earnings Per Share (EPS) Growth Rate
summary: Calculates the percentage change in a company's Earnings Per Share (EPS) from a previous period to the current period, indicating its growth or decline.
applicable_when: Assessing a company's profitability trend over time.
Evaluating the growth or decline in earnings available to each share.
Comparing the financial performance of a company between two specific periods.
Analyzing how efficiently a company converts revenue into per-share profit.
not_applicable_when: You need the actual Price/Earnings-to-Growth (PEG) Ratio.
You only have EPS data for a single period.
You are looking for absolute EPS values rather than a rate of change.
You need to calculate compound annual growth over multiple periods.
You are interested in overall company revenue growth, not per-share earnings.
scope_boundaries: Requires two distinct Earnings Per Share (EPS) values: one for the current period and one for a previous period.
Outputs a percentage value representing the growth or decline.
Focuses strictly on earnings on a per-share basis.
Applies to financial analysis of publicly traded companies or businesses with defined share structures.

common_confusions.label: Price/Earnings-to-Growth (PEG) Ratio
common_confusions.difference: The PEG Ratio compares a company's P/E to its expected EPS growth to assess valuation. This card calculates only the direct period-over-period percentage change in EPS, not the PEG ratio.

common_confusions.label: Earnings Per Share (EPS)
common_confusions.difference: This card computes the *growth rate* of EPS between two periods, not the raw EPS value for a single period.

common_confusions.label: Net Income Growth Rate
common_confusions.difference: This calculation is specifically for *per-share* earnings growth. Net income growth rate measures the growth of total company profit.

common_confusions.label: Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR)
common_confusions.difference: This formula calculates simple period-over-period growth. CAGR is used for smoothed average annual growth over multiple years.

title: Income Annuity
summary: Calculates the fixed monthly payment amount for an immediate annuity given a lump sum premium, annual interest rate, and payout duration in years.
applicable_when: You need to determine the fixed monthly income from a lump sum immediate annuity.
You are planning retirement income streams from a one-time premium.
You want to calculate a guaranteed periodic cash flow over a set period.
not_applicable_when: You are looking for payments from a deferred annuity (payments start later).
You need to calculate payments for an annuity with variable or investment-linked returns.
You are calculating the total future value or accumulated sum of an annuity.
You are interested in the total return of an annuity.
scope_boundaries: Calculates fixed monthly payments only.
Assumes payments are made at the end of each period by default.
Requires a lump sum premium, annual interest rate, and payout duration in years.
Does not account for inflation adjustments to payments.

common_confusions.label: Immediate Annuity vs. Deferred Annuity
common_confusions.difference: This card calculates for an *immediate* annuity, where payments begin soon after the lump sum premium is paid. It is not for *deferred* annuities, where payments start at a future date.

common_confusions.label: Fixed Annuity vs. Variable Annuity
common_confusions.difference: This card calculates a *fixed* monthly payment. It does not account for variable annuities where payments fluctuate based on underlying investment performance.

common_confusions.label: Annuity Payment Timing (End vs. Beginning of Period)
common_confusions.difference: By default, this calculation assumes payments are made at the *end* of each month (ordinary annuity). Payments made at the *beginning* of the month (annuity due) would result in a slightly different payment amount.

title: Bell Curve Data Percentage within One Standard Deviation
summary: Calculates the actual percentage of provided data points that fall within one standard deviation from the mean in a given dataset. This card applies the concept of the empirical rule for normal distributions to concrete data.
selection_hints: Data point analysis relative to mean
applicable_when: You have a dataset and its mean/standard deviation.
You need to know the proportion of data points falling within one standard deviation of the mean.
You are working with data assumed to be normally distributed or want to test its adherence to the empirical rule for one standard deviation.
Analyzing financial security returns or market sensitivity where normal distribution is a factor.
Assessing the spread of performance metrics or test scores.
not_applicable_when: Your data is definitively not normally distributed, as the 'bell curve' interpretation won't hold.
You only need the theoretical 68% from the empirical rule without checking actual data.
You need to calculate percentages for two or three standard deviations, not just one.
You do not have a list of actual data points, only summary statistics (mean, std dev).
You are looking for probabilities of specific values, rather than the percentage of actual observations within a range.
scope_boundaries: Calculates percentage for *one* standard deviation only.
Requires 'mean', 'standard deviation', and a list of 'data_points' as inputs.
The output is the exact calculated percentage from the provided data, not the theoretical 68%.
Results are rounded to two decimal places.
Interpretation as a 'bell curve' is most appropriate for normally distributed data.

common_confusions.label: Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7 Rule)
common_confusions.difference: This card *calculates* the actual percentage of data within one standard deviation based on your specific dataset, whereas the Empirical Rule *states* that 68% of data *should* fall within one standard deviation for a perfectly normal distribution.

common_confusions.label: Percentage within Two or Three Standard Deviations
common_confusions.difference: This card only calculates the percentage of data points within *one* standard deviation. Separate calculations are needed for two or three standard deviations.

common_confusions.label: Theoretical Normal Distribution Probability
common_confusions.difference: This card quantifies the actual distribution of *your specific dataset*. It does not calculate theoretical probabilities using a probability density function for a perfectly normal distribution.

common_confusions.label: Chebyshev's Inequality
common_confusions.difference: Chebyshev's Inequality provides a *lower bound* for any distribution. This card applies specifically to assessing characteristics of (assumed) normal distributions, providing an *exact calculated percentage* for 1 standard deviation from your data.

title: TFSA Over-Contribution Tax Penalty Calculation
summary: Calculates the monthly tax penalty for over-contributions to a Canadian Tax-Free Savings Account (TFSA) based on annual limits.
applicable_when: Calculating tax penalties for exceeding TFSA contribution limits.
Determining penalties for Canadian Tax-Free Savings Account over-contributions.
Assessing financial consequences of TFSA overages in Canada.
Reviewing past TFSA contributions against annual limits for penalty assessment.
not_applicable_when: Calculating general TFSA contribution room or limits without over-contributions.
Determining penalties for other types of registered accounts (e.g., RRSP, RESP).
Evaluating investment accounts outside of Canada.
Seeking general information on TFSA benefits or investment strategies.
No TFSA over-contributions have occurred.
scope_boundaries: Applies specifically to Canadian Tax-Free Savings Accounts (TFSA).
Calculates a 1% monthly penalty on over-contributed amounts.
Requires annual contribution data and corresponding annual TFSA limits as inputs.
Focuses solely on tax penalty calculation, not general TFSA account management.
Does not automatically factor in unused contribution room carried forward from previous years unless specified.

common_confusions.label: Penalty duration (full year vs. actual)
common_confusions.difference: This card assumes penalties accrue for a full year for any year an over-contribution occurred, not based on the actual duration the over-contribution existed before withdrawal.

common_confusions.label: TFSA contribution room (annual vs. carry-forward)
common_confusions.difference: This card calculates over-contributions based on the annual limit only, without automatically including unused contribution room carried forward from previous years.

common_confusions.label: Contribution types (all inclusive vs. specific exclusions)
common_confusions.difference: This card considers all provided contributions as counting towards the annual limit. It does not automatically exclude qualifying transfers or exempt contributions.

title: Long Straddle Profit Calculation
summary: Calculates the potential profit or loss for a long straddle options strategy based on the underlying asset's price movement direction (up or down) at expiration. It accounts for the asset's expiration price, the common strike price, and the total net premium paid for both options.
applicable_when: Anticipating significant price movement (up or down) of an underlying asset
Expecting high volatility in the market due to an imminent event (e.g., earnings report, news)
Aiming to profit from a strong move in either direction by the underlying asset
When a trader believes the underlying asset will move from a low volatility state to a high volatility state
not_applicable_when: Anticipating the underlying asset's price to remain stable or move minimally
When the underlying asset's price is expected to be exactly at the strike price at expiration (maximum loss scenario)
When seeking profits from low volatility or time decay
If the market fails to react strongly enough to an anticipated event
scope_boundaries: Calculates profit/loss on a per-share basis by default.
Requires knowing the underlying asset's price at expiration.
Assumes the same strike price and expiration date for both the call and put options.
Considers the combined net premium paid for both the call and put options.
Excludes trading commissions from profit calculation by default.

common_confusions.label: Profit calculation unit (per share vs. per contract)
common_confusions.difference: This formula defaults to per-share profit calculation. It can be adjusted to per-contract (100 shares) if explicitly specified, which will multiply the result by 100.

common_confusions.label: Inclusion of trading commissions
common_confusions.difference: The calculation by default excludes trading commissions. To include commissions, an additional input for 'commission_per_share' would be required and subtracted from the profit.

common_confusions.label: Long Straddle vs. Short Straddle
common_confusions.difference: A Long Straddle profits from large price movements in either direction (volatility increase), while a Short Straddle profits from minimal price movement and time decay (volatility decrease), aiming for the underlying asset to stay near the strike price.

title: Covered Interest Rate Parity

Analyzing currency markets under a no-arbitrage assumption.
Evaluating covered foreign exchange transactions.
Understanding the relationship between spot rates, forward rates, and interest rate differentials.
When hedging foreign exchange risk using forward contracts.
not_applicable_when: Forecasting future spot exchange rates without using forward contracts.
Seeking to identify and exploit actual arbitrage opportunities.
When foreign exchange risk is not covered or hedged.
When dealing with uncovered interest rate parity.
scope_boundaries: Assumes a perfect capital market with no transaction costs.
Applies a no-arbitrage condition for currency and interest rate markets.
Requires spot exchange rate, domestic, and foreign interest rates as inputs.
Focuses on the relationship involving forward contracts.
Interest rates are annual and should be provided as decimals.

common_confusions.label: Uncovered Interest Rate Parity (UIP)
common_confusions.difference: CIP explicitly involves using forward contracts to hedge foreign exchange risk and assumes no arbitrage. UIP forecasts future spot rates without hedging and does not use forward contracts.

title: Compound Interest
summary: Calculates the total compound interest earned or paid on an initial principal amount over a specified period, considering a fixed compounding frequency.
applicable_when: You need to calculate interest on an investment where interest is added to the principal periodically.
Analyzing the growth of a savings account, fixed deposit, or bond.
Determining the total interest paid on a loan with periodic compounding.
Comparing investment options based on their compounding frequency.
not_applicable_when: You are looking for simple interest, which does not compound.
Interest is compounded continuously (i.e., infinite compounding periods).
The interest rate or principal amount changes over the investment term.
You need to calculate the future value or total amount, not just the interest.
scope_boundaries: Assumes a constant annual interest rate.
Assumes a fixed principal amount without additional contributions or withdrawals.
Requires a specific, discrete compounding frequency (e.g., annually, semi-annually, monthly, quarterly).
Input 'rate' is an annual rate expressed as a decimal (e.g., 0.05 for 5%).
Input 'time' is in years.

common_confusions.label: Simple Interest
common_confusions.difference: Unlike simple interest, which is only on the principal, compound interest is calculated on both the initial principal and the accumulated interest from previous periods.

common_confusions.label: Continuous Compounding
common_confusions.difference: This formula applies to discrete compounding periods (e.g., daily, monthly, annually), not continuous compounding, which involves an infinite number of compounding periods.

common_confusions.label: Future Value
common_confusions.difference: This formula outputs only the *interest earned*. If you need the *total amount* at the end of the period (principal + interest), use a Future Value formula.

common_confusions.label: Effective Annual Rate (EAR)
common_confusions.difference: EAR represents the actual annual rate after accounting for compounding. This formula calculates interest using the nominal annual rate and compounding frequency.

title: Tax Benefit Calculation
summary: Calculates the final tax liability after applying a tax credit, differentiating between refundable and nonrefundable credits.
applicable_when: You need to determine the remaining tax liability after applying a credit.
You want to understand the impact of a refundable versus nonrefundable tax credit.
You are calculating the net tax position, including potential refunds.
not_applicable_when: You are calculating tax deductions, exemptions, or other non-credit tax benefits.
You need to determine the initial tax liability itself, not its reduction by a credit.
You are looking for general tax planning advice beyond credit application.
scope_boundaries: Focuses specifically on the application of tax credits to reduce tax liability.
Accounts for whether a credit can generate a refund (refundable) or only reduce liability to zero (nonrefundable).
Assumes the initial tax liability and credit amount are known.

common_confusions.label: Tax Credit vs. Tax Deduction
common_confusions.difference: This card calculates tax credits (direct reduction of tax owed), not tax deductions (which reduce taxable income). A tax credit directly lowers the amount of tax you owe, while a deduction reduces the income on which your tax is calculated.

common_confusions.label: Net Tax Position vs. Minimum Zero Owed
common_confusions.difference: The result can be a negative value, indicating a refund if the credit is refundable and exceeds the liability. If you only want to see the amount still owed (a minimum of zero), a different calculation is needed for output interpretation.

title: Zero-Volatility Spread (Z-spread)
summary: Calculates the zero-volatility spread (Z-spread) of a bond, which is the constant spread added to the Treasury spot rates that makes the present value of a bond's cash flows equal to its current market price. It quantifies the credit risk premium.
applicable_when: You need to assess the credit risk premium of a bond.
You want to compare a bond's yield to the entire Treasury spot rate curve, not just a single benchmark.
The question explicitly asks for the 'Z-spread' or 'zero-volatility spread'.
You are evaluating a bond without embedded options.
not_applicable_when: You need to account for embedded options (e.g., call/put features) and interest rate volatility.
You only need a simple spread over a single benchmark government bond (e.g., G-spread).
You are interested in a single discount rate like Yield to Maturity (YTM) for a simple comparison.
The instrument is not a fixed-income bond.
scope_boundaries: Requires bond price, all future cash flows, corresponding Treasury spot rates, and the periods to each cash flow.
Calculates a single, constant spread applicable across the entire Treasury spot rate curve.
Assumes the bond has no embedded options (e.g., callable, putable).

common_confusions.label: Option-Adjusted Spread (OAS)
common_confusions.difference: Z-spread assumes no embedded options and a static interest rate environment, while OAS accounts for the impact of embedded options and potential interest rate volatility.

common_confusions.label: Yield to Maturity (YTM)
common_confusions.difference: YTM is a single discount rate that equates a bond's price to its cash flows, whereas Z-spread is a constant spread added to each point on the Treasury spot rate curve.

common_confusions.label: G-Spread (Government Spread)
common_confusions.difference: G-spread is the spread of a bond's yield over a specific benchmark government bond (e.g., a Treasury of similar maturity), whereas Z-spread is the spread over the entire government spot rate curve.

title: Home Mortgage Interest Deduction Tax Savings
summary: Calculates the federal income tax savings realized by itemizing deductions, specifically considering the impact of mortgage interest, compared to claiming the standard deduction.
applicable_when: Comparing the tax impact of itemized deductions vs. standard deduction.
Evaluating the tax benefit of mortgage interest and other itemized deductions.
When itemized deductions exceed the standard deduction.
For federal income tax calculations related to homeownership.
not_applicable_when: Only interested in the total mortgage interest paid, not its tax impact.
When claiming the standard deduction and not itemizing.
When itemized deductions are less than or equal to the standard deduction.
For state or local tax calculations where rules differ.
scope_boundaries: Focuses on federal income tax implications.
Assumes itemized deductions are provided after applying all relevant tax law limits (e.g., mortgage principal limits).
Calculates based on a single marginal tax bracket.
Limited to the tax benefit from the difference between itemized and standard deductions.

common_confusions.label: Tax Bracket Input Format
common_confusions.difference: The tax bracket should be entered as a decimal (e.g., 0.24), not a percentage (e.g., 24).

common_confusions.label: Mortgage Interest Deduction Limits
common_confusions.difference: The provided 'itemized_deductions' should already reflect current tax law limits on mortgage interest (e.g., $750k principal limit), not the raw interest amount.

common_confusions.label: Raw Mortgage Interest vs. Tax Benefit
common_confusions.difference: This calculation provides the tax savings from itemizing, not the total mortgage interest amount paid.

title: Median
summary: Calculates the central value of a sorted list of numbers. It identifies the midpoint of a dataset, useful for understanding data distribution, especially when outliers may distort the average.
applicable_when: Determining the midpoint of a dataset.
Analyzing data with potential outliers or skewed distributions.
Evaluating investment performance or return rates.
Needing a measure of central tendency robust to extreme values.
Comparing against mean or mode for a complete statistical overview.
not_applicable_when: The arithmetic average (mean) is specifically required.
The most frequent value (mode) is needed.
The input list of numbers is empty.
Analyzing data for sum or cumulative effects.
scope_boundaries: Input must be a list of numerical values.
Each element in the input list must be an integer or float.
Output is a single numerical value representing the median.
Assumes input values are typically in a reasonable range, e.g., for percentages.

common_confusions.label: Mean (Average)
common_confusions.difference: The mean is the sum of all values divided by the count, whereas the median is the middle value in a sorted list. The median is less affected by outliers than the mean.

common_confusions.label: Mode
common_confusions.difference: The mode is the most frequently occurring value in a dataset, not necessarily the middle value.

common_confusions.label: Range
common_confusions.difference: The range is the difference between the highest and lowest values, indicating data spread, not a measure of central tendency.
